---
title: "3.6 The Danger of Data Errors"
subtitle: "Chapter 03. Implementing Mean Reversion Strategies"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

# Data Errors Systematically Favor Mean-Reversion Backtests

Chan treats data quality as a special danger for mean-reversion strategies
because outliers in the data often look exactly like the temporary deviations a
mean-reversion system is designed to trade.

If the true trade prices were

$$
100,\ 100,\ 100
$$

but the data record

$$
100,\ 110,\ 100,
$$

then a mean-reversion strategy may short at the false spike and cover at the
next price, booking a fictitious profit. The strategy looks brilliant precisely
because the error mimics a perfect reversal.

# Why Spread Strategies Are Even More Sensitive

For a spread trade, the signal is often a small difference between two large
prices. That makes percentage distortion much worse.

If the true prices are:

- $X = 100$
- $Y = 105$

then the true spread is $Y-X = 5$. If a data error pushes $Y$ to $106$, the
apparent spread becomes $6$. That is only about a 1 percent error on one price,
but a 20 percent error on the spread.

This is why pairs and arbitrage systems can be triggered by very small quote
errors in one leg.

# The Same Mechanism Pollutes Backtests and Live Trading

The historical danger is fake alpha. The live danger is a real trade triggered
by a bad bid or ask quote that never represented executable value. Chan notes
that he observed this directly in live pair trading until he switched to a
better data source.

The practical lesson is that mean-reversion systems need unusually aggressive
data cleaning and quote validation. Without that, the strategy will repeatedly
confuse bad ticks with opportunities.

# Review Checklist

- Could one bad print create a complete false trade in backtest?
- Is the signal a spread small enough that quote noise is magnified?
- Are cancel-and-correct codes or other data-cleaning steps present?
- Is the live feed trustworthy enough for spread-triggered execution?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [3.5 Kalman Filter as Market-Making Model]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-03/sections/05-kalman-filter-as-market-making-model/)
- Next: [Chapter overview]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/)
